content:
3 min read Just now -- 1. Introduction In the previous article, we learned how to navigate between pages using routerLink and display components using router-outlet. In this article, we’ll learn: • Default Route (path: '') • routerLinkActive • routerLinkActiveOptions We’ll also move our navigation bar into a separate Header Component to keep the application organized. 2. What We Will Build Press enter or click to view image in full size Our application will have: • Header Component • Home Page • About Page • Contact Page • Login Page The active menu item will automatically be highlighted. 3. Create Components ng generate component header ng generate component home ng generate component about ng generate component contact ng generate component login4. Configure Routes app.routes.ts import { Routes } from '@angular/router';import { HomeComponent } from './home/home.component'; import { AboutComponent } from './about/about.component'; import { ContactComponent } from './contact/contact.component'; import { LoginComponent } from './login/login.component'; export const routes: Routes = [ { path:'', component:HomeComponent }, { path:'about', component:AboutComponent }, { path:'contact', component:ContactComponent }, { path:'login', component:LoginComponent } ]; Definition The default route (path: '') tells Angular which component to display when the application first loads. 5. Add Header Component Instead of placing navigation inside app.component.html, we'll move it into a separate Header Component. app.component.html 6. Header Component HTML header.component.html
Home About Contact Login
7. Import Required Modules header.component.ts import { Component } from '@angular/core'; import { RouterLink, RouterLinkActive } from '@angular/router';@Component({ selector: 'app-header', standalone: true, imports: [RouterLink, RouterLinkActive], templateUrl: './header.component.html', styleUrl: './header.component.css' }) export class HeaderComponent { } 8. What is routerLinkActive? routerLinkActive automatically adds a CSS class to the link when its route is active. Example: About When the About page is open, Angular automatically adds the active class. Result: About9. What is routerLinkActiveOptions? routerLinkActiveOptions changes how Angular decides whether a link is active. Example: Home 10. Why do we use exact: true? Without exact:true When you open /about Angular also considers the Home route ("") active because every URL starts with an empty path.Both links become active. With [routerLinkActiveOptions]=”{exact:true}” Home becomes active only when the URL is exactly:/ 11. Add CSS header.component.css nav { display: flex; gap: 20px; padding: 15px; background: #2c3e50; }nav a { color: white; text-decoration: none; padding: 8px 15px; border-radius: 5px; } nav a:hover { background: #3498db; } .active { background: #16a085; } 12. Import HeaderComponent app.component.ts import { Component } from '@angular/core'; import { RouterOutlet } from '@angular/router'; import { HeaderComponent } from './header/header.component';@Component({ selector: 'app-root', standalone: true, imports: [RouterOutlet,HeaderComponent], templateUrl: './app.component.html', styleUrl: './app.component.css' }) export class AppComponent { title = 'my-angular-app7'; } 13. How It Works Application Starts │ ▼ Default Route ("") Loads │ ▼ Home Component Opens │ ▼ User Clicks Navigation Link │ ▼ routerLink Changes URL │ ▼ Angular Checks Active Route │ ▼ routerLinkActive Adds "active" Class │ ▼ Selected Link Gets Highlighted14. Conclusion In this article, we learned how to: • Create a default route using path: '' • Move navigation into a reusable Header Component • Highlight the active navigation link using routerLinkActive • Prevent multiple active links by using routerLinkActiveOptions with exact: true
